topic: Someone finds an unsigned birthday card in their mailbox
raw_output:
[story 1]

The mail piled up on the kitchen counter, a chaotic landscape of bills and junk flyers. I picked up a thick, cream-colored card, its edges slightly softened. It was addressed simply to 'Alex' and contained a meticulously drawn watercolor of a sunset over the ocean. My breath hitched. It was a birthday card. A proper, thoughtful one. I traced the delicate lines with my thumb, a tight knot forming in my chest. I glanced towards the living room where Sarah was laughing, a genuine, unrestrained sound that seemed to vibrate in the air. How could someone receive such attention? I shoved the card deep into the bottom of my drawer, the paper feeling suddenly heavy and significant. I didn't want to look at it again.

[story 2]

It was tucked between a utility notice and a glossy advertisement for a new car. The card was pristine, the script elegant and looping. My fingers, usually steady, trembled slightly as I unfolded it. Inside, a heartfelt message about shared memories and future hopes. A sudden, sharp clenching sensation tightened my jaw. I stared at the paper, the vibrant colors of the illustration mocking the muted tones of my own room. I felt a distinct tightening in my throat, a sudden desire to snatch the card away and hide it somewhere inaccessible. I quickly smoothed the card flat and slid it into a seldom-used box, hoping the silence inside would somehow absorb the feeling of being overlooked.

[story 3]

The afternoon light slanted across the porch, illuminating the contents of the mailbox. There, nestled amongst the usual refuse, was a card. It was addressed to 'Jamie,' and the artwork was breathtakingly intricate—a detailed depiction of a starry night. I picked it up, the paper cool beneath my fingertips. A strange, hollow feeling settled over me, like a sudden drop in temperature. I looked over at my phone, seeing notifications pop up—friends celebrating milestones, sending elaborate greetings. A small, tight smile played on my lips, quickly suppressed. I folded the card with unnecessary precision, tucking it into my wallet, a secret weight I carried as I walked back inside.
